key_vocabulary.significance: צְדָקָה (ṣĕdāqāh, ‘rightness, rectitude — especially of a judge or ruler; hence justice, equity’) is half of Amos’s most-quoted verse: Amos 5:24: “But let justice (mišpāṭ) roll down like waters, and righteousness (ṣĕdāqāh) like an ever-flowing stream.” MLK quoted this verse in his “I Have a Dream” speech (1963); it has become the OT’s most recognized statement of social justice. But in its Amos context, ṣĕdāqāh is covenant behavior — the specific conduct expected of those in covenant with YHWH, not generic moral goodness. It is the alternative to everything Amos condemns in chapters 3–5: the luxurious lifestyle of the northern elite (4:1-3; 6:1-7), the rigged markets (8:4-6), and above all the pious religious assemblies that YHWH says he hates (5:21-23). The “ever-flowing stream” (naḥal ʾēṯān — a perennial wadi rather than a seasonal one that dries in summer) implies that ṣĕdāqāh must be constant — present in the everyday marketplace, not only in moments of solemn assembly. The ṣĕdāqāh Amos calls for is the behavior of the courts (give the evyon a fair hearing), the marketplace (accurate weights), and the harvest (leave gleaning for the poor). The NT’s “do not merely listen to the word, and so deceive yourselves — do what it says” (Jas 1:22) and James’s critique of faith without deeds (Jas 2:14-17) stand in direct continuity with Amos’s ṣĕdāqāh critique of disconnected religion.

הוֹי (hôy, ‘alas! woe! ha! — an interjection of grief and warning, borrowed from the funeral cry over the dead’) is the opening word of Amos’s most devastating oracles. Amos 5:18: “Woe (hôy) to you who desire the day of the LORD!” — Israel was expecting the yôm YHWH as the day YHWH would defeat their enemies and vindicate them. Amos reverses this: the Day will be darkness for Israel, not light (5:18-20: “Is not the day of the LORD darkness, and not light, and gloom with no brightness in it?”). Amos 6:1: “Woe (hôy) to those who are at ease in Zion, and to those who feel secure on the mountain of Samaria, the notable men of the first of the nations, to whom the house of Israel comes!” The hôy oracle against Zion’s complacent elite (6:1-7) is Amos’s most socially specific — ivory-inlaid couches, choice meats, improvised songs, bowls of wine — the prosperity that should have produced gratitude and justice has produced self-indulgence and indifference. The hôy formula is borrowed from the funeral cry over the dead (Jer 22:18: “They shall not lament for him, saying, ‘Ah, my brother!’ or ‘Ah, sister!’”). By opening an oracle against the living with the funeral cry, Amos announces that the comfortable are already dead — already mourned — without knowing it. The NT’s “Woe to you who are rich, for you have received your consolation” (Luke 6:24) and “Woe to you who are well fed now” (6:25) directly inhabit this Amos-hôy reversal.

אֲנָךְ (ʾănāk, ‘a plumb-line — related to Akkadian anāku, meaning “lead”’) is unique to Amos 7:7-8 in all of the OT — there is no other occurrence of this word in all of Hebrew Scripture. The word appears four times in two verses: “Behold, the Lord was standing beside a wall built with a plumb line (ʾănāk), with a plumb line in his hand. And the LORD said to me, ‘Amos, what do you see?’ And I said, ‘A plumb line (ʾănāk).’ Then the Lord said, ‘Behold, I am setting a plumb line (ʾănāk) in the midst of my people Israel.’” A plumb line is a builder’s tool for checking whether a wall is truly vertical: it passes judgment not by comparison to another wall but against the absolute vertical of gravity. YHWH’s ʾănāk is the standard of covenant faithfulness against which Israel’s life is measured — and found crooked. The image is theologically precise: the wall was built with a plumb line (bĕʾănāk — originally vertical, originally faithful). Israel was not always crooked; she was built straight. What Amos announces is a deviation from an original alignment, not an original deficiency. The wall has leaned out. The ʾănāk-vision is the third of Amos’s five visions, and it is the one where YHWH announces there will be no more pardoning (7:8: “I will never again pass by them”) — the measurement has been taken, the verdict rendered.

סֻכָּה (sukkāh, ‘a shelter made of branches — a hut or booth; the temporary shelters used during the Feast of Tabernacles’) appears in Amos’s pivotal restoration oracle: Amos 9:11: “In that day I will raise up the booth (sukkāh) of David that is fallen and repair its breaches, and raise up its ruins and rebuild it as in the days of old.” The sukkāh of David is a deliberately diminished image for the Davidic dynasty: not a cedar palace (bayit — house, as in 2 Sam 7) but a fallen, damaged sukkāh — a leaky temporary shelter. The Davidic covenant is not destroyed but dilapidated; the promise is not a new foundation but the raising up (qûm) of what has collapsed. James cites Amos 9:11-12 (LXX) in Acts 15:16-17 as the scriptural basis for accepting Gentiles without circumcision: “After this I will return, and I will rebuild the tent of David that has fallen... so that the rest of mankind may seek the Lord, and all the Gentiles who are called by my name.” The rebuilt sukkāh of David is identified with the resurrection of Jesus and the multiethnic church — the nations who bear YHWH’s name in Amos 9:12 are the nations Peter and Paul are finding in the Gentile mission. The sukkāh’s Tabernacles resonance is also significant: the Feast of Sukkot celebrates Israel’s wilderness sojourn, where YHWH sheltered his people in temporary booths. The restored sukkāh is a return to the intimacy of desert dependency.

דַּל (dal, ‘lean, weak, thin — of poor people reduced by circumstances to physical thinness from lack of food and resources’) is one of two words Amos uses for the economically vulnerable (alongside evyon, H34 — needy). Amos 2:7: “those who trample the head of the poor (dal) into the dust of the earth.” Amos 4:1: “you cows of Bashan... who oppress the poor (dal), who crush the needy (ʾevyôn).” Amos 5:11: “because you trample on the poor (dal) and you exact taxes of grain from him, you have built houses of hewn stone, but you shall not dwell in them.” The word dal describes not just poverty but extreme physical frailty — people without any reserves who cannot absorb further loss. The covenant context is essential: Deuteronomy commanded Israel to open their hands to the dal (Deut 15:7-11) and not to harden their hearts. Amos’s indictment is that the covenant community has done the exact opposite — they have built luxury houses (hewn stone was expensive; most houses were mudbrick) on the crushed bodies of the dal. The prophetic curse matches the crime: the hewn-stone houses will not be inhabited (Amos 5:11) — the labor of oppression will not enjoy its fruit. The NT’s “blessed are the poor” (Luke 6:20) and the early church’s care for the poor (Gal 2:10: “they asked us to remember the poor, the very thing I was eager to do”) stand in direct continuity with Amos’s dal-theology.

אֶבְיוֹן (ʾevyôn, ‘a beggar, one in want — from ʾāvāh, to desire or long for what one needs’) appears across Amos as the second term in the dal/ʾevyôn pair, together constituting Amos’s full vocabulary for the economically destroyed. Amos 2:6: “they sell the righteous for silver, and the needy (ʾevyôn) for a pair of sandals.” The price is startling — a person’s legal standing has been sold for the price of footwear, the most basic commodity. Amos 5:12: “you turn aside the needy (ʾevyôn) in the gate” — the city gate was where ANE courts met to hear cases; the ʾevyôn is being denied justice in the very place designated for its administration. Amos 8:4-6: “Hear this, you who trample on the needy (ʾevyôn)... saying, ‘When will the new moon be over, that we may sell grain? And the Sabbath, that we may offer wheat for sale, that we may make the ephah small and the shekel great and deal deceitfully with false balances, that we may buy the poor (dal) for silver and the ʾevyôn for a pair of sandals and sell the refuse of the wheat?’” The ʾevyôn-oracle of 8:4-6 is Amos’s most specific economic indictment: the merchants observe the Sabbath and the new moon festivals while counting the minutes until they can resume their exploitative commerce. Religion and exploitation coexist without tension — until Amos arrives. The NT’s James echoes this directly: “you have lived on the earth in luxury and in self-indulgence... you have condemned and murdered the righteous person” (Jas 5:5-6).

רָעָב (rāʿāv, ‘hunger, dearth, famine — from rāʿēv, to be hungry’) appears in Amos’s most haunting oracle: Amos 8:11: “Behold, the days are coming — declares the Lord GOD — when I will send a famine (rāʿāv) on the land: not a famine of bread, nor a thirst for water, but of hearing the words of the LORD.” The oracle redefines famine from physical to revelatory: the most devastating scarcity is not agricultural but prophetic — the withdrawal of the divine word. This is the inverse of Amos’s entire ministry: the prophet who has spoken YHWH’s word throughout the book announces that the word will be taken away. The rāʿāv of 8:11 is covenant judgment at its most fundamental: Israel commanded the prophets to be silent (Amos 2:12: “you commanded the prophets, saying, ‘You shall not prophesy’”), and now silence will be given. The rāʿāv also explains why Amos’s restoration vision (9:11-15) is so climactic: the reversal of the word-famine is the reversal of the most fundamental deprivation. The NT’s “man does not live by bread alone, but by every word that comes from the mouth of God” (Matt 4:4, citing Deut 8:3) assumes the same rāʿāv-theology: there is a hunger deeper than physical hunger, and only the divine word satisfies it.

צָמֵא (ṣāmēʾ, ‘to be thirsty; also as adjective, thirsty’) pairs with rāʿāv (famine) in Amos 8:11 to describe the spiritual deprivation that is Israel’s ultimate judgment: “not a famine of bread, nor a thirst (ṣāmēʾ) for water, but of hearing the words of the LORD.” The rāʿāv/ṣāmēʾ pair (famine/thirst) is a standard ANE merism for total deprivation: without food and without water, death is certain. Amos 8:12-13 develops the image: “They shall wander from sea to sea, and from north to east; they shall run to and fro, to seek the word of the LORD, but they shall not find it. In that day the lovely virgins and the young men shall faint for thirst (ṣāmēʾ).” The wandering from sea to sea and the ṣāmēʾ of the young together evoke the wilderness: Israel in the desert, thirsty and dying, seeking water — but now without the divine presence that made the desert endurable. Joel 3:18 had promised that mountains would drip with wine and the valleys with milk in the restoration; Amos’s ṣāmēʾ-oracle is the anti-Joel, the withdrawal of all that moisture. The NT’s “if anyone thirsts (dipsaō), let him come to me and drink” (John 7:37, spoken at the Feast of Tabernacles) reverses Amos’s ṣāmēʾ directly: the Spirit poured out by Christ is the end of the rāʿāv/ṣāmēʾ for the divine word.

גּוֹי (gôy, ‘a foreign nation, a people — especially a non-Israelite people; a Gentile’) frames both the opening and the restoration of Amos. Amos 1–2 structures judgment as oracles against the gôyim — Damascus, Gaza, Tyre, Edom, Ammon, Moab, Judah, and finally Israel — building a rhetorical trap: the Israelite audience initially approves of YHWH’s judgment on the surrounding gôyim before being included in the indictment themselves. The rhetorical structure is brilliant: each oracle uses the same formula (“for three transgressions of X, for four, I will not revoke the punishment”) and escalates geographically toward Israel. By the time Amos reaches Israel in 2:6, the audience has endorsed the same standard they are now being measured by. Amos 9:12: “that they may possess the remnant of Edom and all the nations (gôyim) who are called by my name.” The restoration oracle includes the gôyim — the very peoples who were the objects of judgment in chapters 1–2 are now, in 9:12, included as bearers of YHWH’s name. James cites 9:11-12 in Acts 15:17 (LXX: “all the Gentiles upon whom my name has been called”) to justify accepting Gentiles into the church. The gôy-arc in Amos is among the most dramatic in the OT: from instruments of judgment (1–2) to objects of condemnation (6–7) to beneficiaries of restoration (9:12).

קַיִץ (qayiṣ, ‘summer; harvest; summer fruit — the ripe fruit gathered at the end of the agricultural year, before autumn’) is the visual object of Amos’s fourth vision, which turns entirely on a wordplay: Amos 8:1-2: “This is what the Lord GOD showed me: behold, a basket of summer fruit (qayiṣ). And he said, ‘Amos, what do you see?’ And I said, ‘A basket of summer fruit (qayiṣ).’ Then the LORD said to me, ‘The end (qēṣ — H7093) has come upon my people Israel.’” The whole point of the vision is the pun: qayiṣ (summer fruit) sounds like qēṣ (end, extremity). Amos sees qayiṣ; YHWH announces qēṣ. The agricultural metaphor carries theological weight: qayiṣ is the ripe fruit at the end of summer’s long growing season — the harvest of what has been developing all year. Israel’s sin has been ripening all season; the time of harvest is now. The basket of qayiṣ is full — ready to be consumed and finished. Amos 8:3 continues: “The songs of the temple shall become wailings in that day — many dead bodies! — they are thrown everywhere. Silence!” The abundance of the harvest basket (full of ripe fruit) becomes the abundance of corpses. The NT’s “the harvest is the end of the age” (Matt 13:39) and the winepress of God’s wrath (Rev 14:19-20) develop this qayiṣ-judgment tradition.

קֵץ (qēṣ, ‘an extremity — the outer edge; adverbially, at the end; used of the end of a divinely appointed period’) is the punch-word of the qayiṣ/qēṣ pun in Amos 8:2: “the end (qēṣ) has come upon my people Israel.” In the prophets, qēṣ refers to the divinely decreed terminus of a period — the end of exile in Daniel (Dan 8:17,19; 11:27,35; 12:4,9), the end of a dynasty, the end of divine patience. Amos 8:2’s qēṣ falls with the weight of irreversibility: this is not merely the end of the agricultural year but the end of YHWH’s forbearance with Israel’s covenant unfaithfulness. The sound of qayiṣ (summer fruit) triggers the announcement of qēṣ — a pleasant image of harvest abundance becomes an oracle of termination. What makes the pun devastating is its indirection: YHWH does not say “the end has come” — he shows Amos a basket of fruit and lets the prophet speak the word (qayiṣ) that announces the judgment (qēṣ). The prophet’s own observation becomes the instrument of his nation’s sentence. The qēṣ in Daniel inherits Amos’s usage: the appointed end of the exile is the divinely decreed completion of a period of judgment, after which restoration begins. The NT’s “the end of the ages has come upon us” (1 Cor 10:11 — ta telē tōn aiōnōn) and “in these last days” (Heb 1:2) continue the qēṣ-theological tradition of the decisive appointed moment.

נָטָה (nāṭāh, ‘to extend, stretch, spread, or incline; causatively, to bend, bow, or turn aside — often in a negative sense, to pervert’) is Amos’s verb for the specific legal crime of perverting justice against the vulnerable. Amos 2:7: “those who turn aside (yattû) the way of the humble.” Amos 5:12: “you who afflict the righteous, who take a bribe, and turn aside (hattôtêm) the needy (ʾevyôn) in the gate.” The “gate” (šaʿar) is the city gate — the public forum where ANE courts held their proceedings. To “turn aside” the ʾevyôn in the gate is to redirect the course of the legal process away from the poor, to cause their case to swerve before it can reach judgment. The image of nāṭāh draws on the metaphor of a straight path that is bent: justice should flow straight from complaint to verdict, but the corrupt judiciary bends it (nāṭāh) so the ʾevyôn never arrives at a fair outcome. The same verb appears in Deut 24:17: “You shall not pervert (lōʾ tatteh) the justice due to the sojourner or to the fatherless.” Amos’s indictment echoes the Deuteronomic code precisely: the protection commanded in Deuteronomy is being violated in the very gate designated for its administration. The NT’s James on favoritism in the assembly (Jas 2:1-9: “have you not made distinctions among yourselves and become judges with evil thoughts?”) continues this nāṭāh-at-the-gate critique into the new covenant community.

language_notes:
Amos’s most brilliant rhetorical device is the Oracles Against the Nations (chapters 1–2), which functions as an elaborate trap. The oracles begin with Damascus (Israel’s northeastern neighbor), move through Gaza, Tyre, Edom, Ammon, and Moab — all enemies with whom Israel’s audience would sympathize — before landing on Judah (a brother nation) and finally on Israel itself. Each oracle opens with the same formula: “For three transgressions of X, and for four, I will not revoke the punishment.” The stepped number formula (three/four) is an ANE rhetorical device for “past the limit of acceptable offenses.” By the time Amos reaches Israel in 2:6-16, the audience has heard seven nations condemned by the same standard they are now being measured by. They have approved the formula; they have endorsed the moral logic; they cannot now deny its application to themselves. The structure is prosecutorial: Amos does not argue for the standard — he lets the audience establish it through their own concurrence before turning it on them.

Three doxological fragments interrupt Amos’s judgment oracles at 4:13, 5:8-9, and 9:5-6, functioning as theological anchors in the flow of condemnation. Amos 4:13: “For behold, he who forms the mountains and creates the wind, and declares to man what is his thought, who makes the morning darkness, and treads on the heights of the earth — the LORD, the God of hosts, is his name!” These hymn-fragments are not interruptions but interpretive keys: the Judge who brings Israel to account is the Creator of mountains and wind, the one who “treads on the heights of the earth.” The hymns prevent the reader from domesticating the judgment. YHWH’s case against Israel is not the complaint of a tribal deity aggrieved by small infractions but the judgment of the Creator of all things against the covenant people he made for himself. The doxologies are syntactically abrupt — they begin in the participial form (the one who..., the one who...) and end with the covenant name “YHWH ṣĕbāʾôt” — the sudden declaration of the divine name closing each hymn with a theological period.

Amos’s five visions (7:1–9:10) escalate in intensity from intercessory pause (locusts, fire — 7:1-6) to unrelenting judgment (plumb line, summer fruit, the destroyed temple — 7:7–9:10). The first two visions (locust swarm, cosmic fire) prompt Amos to intercede — “O Lord GOD, please forgive! How can Jacob stand? He is so small!” — and YHWH relents. But the third vision (the ʾănāk — plumb line) produces no intercession, only a verdict: “I will never again pass by them.” The shift is structural: the first two visions leave room for repentance; the third closes it. The fourth vision (qayiṣ/qēṣ — summer fruit/end) is a pun — the prophet’s own observation of the basket becomes the vehicle of the judgment. The fifth vision (9:1-10: “I saw the Lord standing beside the altar, and he said, ‘Strike the capitals until the thresholds shake’”) is the most terrifying — YHWH himself commands the destruction of his own temple. The escalating visions trace the collapse of the space for prophetic intercession.

Amos’s social justice vocabulary is rooted in the legal register of ANE gate-proceedings. The city gate (šaʿar) was the public forum for legal disputes, commercial transactions, and civic life — the place where society’s formal commitments to equity were either kept or violated. Amos uses technical legal vocabulary throughout: to “turn aside” (nāṭāh — H5186) a case, to “sell” (mākar) the righteous, to “trample” (šāʾap) the poor, to “exact tribute” (śe’et) of grain. The marketplace vocabulary is equally specific: “making the ephah small and the shekel great” (8:5) refers to rigging the grain-measure and the weight-measure simultaneously — cheating on both sides of the transaction. Amos is not speaking in generalities about abstract injustice; he is describing the specific mechanisms by which the northern elite extract wealth from the vulnerable. The prophet who was himself a herdsman and dresser of sycamore figs (7:14) brings an outside observer’s eye to the economic practices of the urban north, naming what the insiders prefer not to name.
reception:
New Testament: The most direct Amos-citation in the NT is James’s use of Amos 9:11-12 in Acts 15:16-17 (LXX), where the rebuilt “tent of David” is identified with the resurrection of Jesus and the inclusion of the Gentiles in the church. This citation settled the Jerusalem Council’s debate about whether Gentiles needed to be circumcised — James argued from Amos that the eschatological inclusion of “all the Gentiles who are called by my name” was always the plan. Amos 5:24 (“let justice roll down like waters”) and Amos 5:25-27 are quoted in Stephen’s speech (Acts 7:42-43), citing Israel’s wilderness idolatry as precedent for current rejection of the Spirit. The NT letter of James reflects Amos’s social ethics throughout (Jas 2:1-7 on favoritism, 5:1-6 on exploitation of the poor) without formal citation.

Patristic and Reformation: Jerome wrote a commentary on Amos that became a standard reference in the medieval West; he treated Amos’s social critique as a permanent prophetic indictment of clerical wealth and ecclesiastical complacency. John Chrysostom drew heavily on Amos in his Antioch homilies against the wealthy — the “cows of Bashan” oracle (Amos 4:1) is cited in his attacks on the luxury of the Antioch aristocracy. Calvin’s Amos commentary emphasized the covenant-accountability structure: election (3:2: “You only have I known”) heightens rather than diminishes responsibility. Luther cited Amos as a forerunner of his own polemic against liturgical formalism disconnected from genuine faith and justice.

Modern: Amos became the foundational prophetic text for liberation theology in the 20th century — Gustavo Gutiérrez and Jon Sobrino treated Amos 5:21-24 as the OT basis for the “preferential option for the poor.” MLK’s citation of Amos 5:24 in his “I Have a Dream” speech (1963) and his “Letter from Birmingham Jail” gave the verse its modern cultural resonance. The scholarship of James Limburg and Shalom Paul has recovered the legal and economic precision of Amos’s indictments, establishing that the prophet is not speaking in generalities but describing specific economic mechanisms.
reading_guide:
Read chapters 1–2 as a deliberate rhetorical experience, not just as background. As you move through the oracles against Damascus, Gaza, Tyre, Edom, Ammon, and Moab, notice your own response — agreement, perhaps satisfaction, that YHWH condemns these nations. When Amos turns to Israel in 2:6, you are in the same position as the original audience: you have already endorsed the standard by which Israel is now being measured. The rhetorical trap is participatory. Amos’s point is not merely that Israel is guilty but that Israel knows the standard — and cannot claim ignorance of it.

Don’t stop at chapter 6. The five visions of chapters 7–9 are not an appendix but the theological resolution of the oracles. The first two visions (locust, fire) show Amos interceding and YHWH relenting; the third (the ʾănāk) shows the space for intercession close. Watch how the relationship between prophet and God changes across the five visions — from intercessor to witness to mere observer of the divine verdict. By 9:1-10, even the temple offers no protection.

Read 9:11-15 with everything that preceded it in mind. The restoration oracle is brief — five verses after eight chapters of judgment — but it carries the weight of everything that has been threatened. The fallen sukkāh of David will be raised; the land will overflow with grain and wine; the exiled people will be replanted and never uprooted. The brevity is deliberate: Amos’s message is primarily judgment, but the final word is restoration. James’s use of this passage in Acts 15 shows how the NT reads Amos: the five verses at the end are the hermeneutical key to the whole — the nations called by YHWH’s name are what the judgment was clearing the way for.
